name: Payment Rules Engine — Built with Full TDD
scenario: Build a payment rules engine from scratch using strict TDD discipline. The engine processes payment requests and applies business rules: fraud detection, limit checks, fee calculation, and retry logic. Every class and method is written after a failing test. No production code without a test.
description: Students build PaymentRulesEngine test-first, showing each TDD cycle explicitly: the failing test, the minimum implementation, and the refactor step where needed. The final system has full test coverage because tests drove every line of production code.

core.description: At least 6 TDD cycles documented in comments: `// RED: test name`, `// GREEN: implementation`, `// REFACTOR: description`. Each cycle adds one behaviour to PaymentRulesEngine.

Tests cover all rules: (1) valid payment accepted, (2) amount <= 0 → REJECTED, (3) amount > 100000 → REQUIRES_REVIEW, (4) fraudScore > 0.8 → BLOCKED, (5) daily limit exceeded → REJECTED, (6) fee calculation (1% of amount, min ₹10).
Triangulation demonstrated: at least two boundary tests for the amount limit rule (99999 → ACCEPTED, 100000 → REQUIRES_REVIEW, 100001 → REQUIRES_REVIEW).
Exception test included: null PaymentRequest throws NullPointerException before any processing.
At least one Refactor step shown: code improved between two cycles without changing behavior (rename, extract method, remove duplication). Tests must remain GREEN before and after.
All tests pass. No production code exists without a corresponding test.

bonus.description: Add retry logic with TDD: PaymentRulesEngine.processWithRetry(request, maxRetries) — if first attempt returns REQUIRES_REVIEW, retry up to maxRetries times. Write failing test, implement, triangulate.
Add accumulator with TDD: track daily totals per user (Map). processDailyLimit(userId, amount) — fails if would exceed ₹500,000 daily limit. Three test cycles: under limit, at limit, over limit.
Add a parameterized test (@CsvSource) that validates all boundary values for the amount limit in one test method. Demonstrate that TDD + parameterized tests work well together.

data_to_use:
Payment rules:
- amount <= 0 → REJECTED (invalid)
- amount > 100,000 → REQUIRES_REVIEW (large transaction)
- fraudScore > 0.8 → BLOCKED (fraud detected)
- User has exceeded daily limit → REJECTED
- Otherwise → ACCEPTED

Fee: max(amount × 0.01, 10.0)

Test data:
Small payment: amount=500, fraudScore=0.2 → ACCEPTED, fee=10.0
Large payment: amount=150000, fraudScore=0.3 → REQUIRES_REVIEW, fee=1500.0
Fraud: amount=5000, fraudScore=0.9 → BLOCKED
Limit exceeded: amount=1000, user at limit → REJECTED

criteria: Each rule has its own test (25 pts)
Boundary values for amount limit (15 pts)
null → NPE tested and implemented (10 pts)
One refactor step documented and safe (10 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Module 12 capstone — no AI. TDD is a discipline, not just a technique. Each cycle must be documented. Key reminder: Green phase = minimum code. If you can return a hardcoded value and pass the test, do that — then triangulate. Don't skip to the final implementation.
submission_instructions: Save as PaymentRulesEngineTest.java + PaymentRulesEngine.java. Push to GitHub with commit message: 'Module 12 TDD capstone: payment rules engine'. The commit history ideally shows one commit per TDD cycle.
tips_for_success: Start with the simplest possible test: valid payment → ACCEPTED
Minimum GREEN: return new PaymentDecision('ACCEPTED', 'OK', 10.0) — hardcoded!
Second test: zero amount → REJECTED. This forces the real if-else
Boundary triangulation: test 99999 AND 100000 — they must return different statuses
Fee: Math.max(amount * 0.01, 10.0) — test both above and below the ₹10 minimum
